name: Minarets & Shadow Lake Loop
summary: A 37.5-mile circuit through the heart of the Minarets, passing Shadow Lake, Ediza Lake, Iceberg Lake, and a string of alpine lakes along the Clark Lakes basin. The route threads beneath the Clyde and Leonard Minarets, visits the historic Minaret Mine, and crosses varied terrain from forested river valleys to exposed granite benches with 15 named lakes along the way.
bestFor: Backpackers who want the highest concentration of named alpine lakes and Minaret views in a single loop without crossing any mountain passes.

segments.trailName: River Trail → Shadow Creek Trail
segments.note: Follow the Middle Fork San Joaquin River past Olaine Lake, then climb along Shadow Creek to the shores of Shadow Lake — one of the most photographed lakes in the Ansel Adams Wilderness.

segments.trailName: John Muir Trail
segments.note: Southbound on the JMT through the famous lake chain: Shadow Lake, Rosalie Lake, Gladys Lake, and Emily Lake in quick succession along Shadow Creek.

segments.trailName: John Muir Trail → Minaret Creek Trail
segments.note: Continue past Johnston Lake and turn onto Minaret Creek Trail, climbing along Minaret Creek with views of Red Top Mountain to the west.

segments.trailName: Minaret Mine Trail
segments.note: Out-and-back spur to the historic Minaret Mine — a well-preserved mining site from the 1800s perched above the creek drainage.

segments.trailName: Minaret Creek Trail → Cecile Lake Trail
segments.note: Descend Minaret Creek beneath Riegelhuth Minaret, then traverse to Iceberg Lake under the sheer face of Clyde Minaret — the tallest of the Minarets at 12,281 ft.

segments.trailName: Shadow Creek Trail
segments.note: Follow Shadow Creek past Ediza Lake with Leonard Minaret towering overhead, then descend the Shadow Creek drainage through forest back toward the river.

segments.trailName: John Muir Trail → Garnet Lake Cutoff → River Trail
segments.note: Brief JMT section to Clarice Lake, then cut north on the Garnet Lake Cutoff past Altha Lake and rejoin the River Trail along the Middle Fork San Joaquin with Badger Lakes visible below.

segments.trailName: Pacific Crest Trail → Spooky Meadow Trail → Clark Lakes Trail
segments.note: Swing through the Clark Lakes basin — Gem Lake, Clark Lakes, and Billy Lake along Rush Creek — then return via Spooky Meadow past Summit Lake to close the loop.

pros: Highest named-feature density of any loop in the wilderness: 15 named lakes plus 4 Minaret peaks on a single circuit
Only 29% of miles on JMT/PCT — most of the route follows quieter trails like Shadow Creek Trail and Minaret Creek Trail
Shadow Lake, Ediza Lake, and Iceberg Lake are three of the most scenic destinations in the Sierra, all on one loop
Multiple viable trailheads including Shadow Creek Trailhead — flexibility if one is full
cons: No mountain pass crossings — the route stays in the valleys and benches, so hikers seeking above-treeline exposure will want the high-passes alternative
The Minaret Mine spur adds 3.4 miles of out-and-back — skipable but it means less new terrain per mile
Shadow Creek Trail between Ediza Lake and the River Trail junction is heavily used by day hikers from Agnew Meadows in July
At 37.5 miles over 4 days, day 1 and day 4 run slightly over 10 miles each
gearTips: Bear canister required in the Ansel Adams Wilderness — no exceptions in July
Mosquito head net essential for Shadow Lake and Ediza Lake camps in early July
Shadow Creek crossings can run high in July snowmelt — trekking poles recommended for fords

name: Donohue Pass & Thousand Island Traverse
summary: A 43.7-mile loop crossing two major Sierra passes — Donohue Pass (11,056 ft) and Island Pass (10,203 ft) — along the spine of the JMT. The route follows Rush Creek to the Donohue Pass approach, circumnavigates Thousand Island Lake, then returns south through the Shadow Lake chain and Clark Lakes basin. Donohue Peak is visible from the pass approach.
bestFor: Experienced intermediate hikers who want real above-treeline pass crossings and the full JMT high-country experience, and are comfortable with 11-mile days.

segments.trailName: Rush Creek Trail → John Muir Trail
segments.note: Climb from the Rush Creek trailhead past Gem Lake and Billy Lake, then ascend the JMT toward Donohue Pass. The 2.8-mile approach crosses snowfields in July with Donohue Peak visible to the northeast. Cross Donohue Pass at 11,056 ft — the highest point on the loop.

segments.trailName: Davis Lake Trail → John Muir Trail → Thousand Island Lake Trail
segments.note: Side trip to Lower Davis Lake, then south over Island Pass past Island Pass Lake. Descend to Thousand Island Lake and circle its granite-studded shore on the Thousand Island Lake Trail — Banner Peak dominates the western skyline.

segments.trailName: John Muir Trail → Pacific Crest Trail → River Trail
segments.note: Drop to the Middle Fork San Joaquin River via the John Muir Trail–Pacific Crest Trail junction and head south on the River Trail past Badger Lakes.

segments.trailName: River Trail → Shadow Creek Trail → John Muir Trail
segments.note: Follow the River Trail past Olaine Lake, then climb Shadow Creek to Shadow Lake. The JMT section between Shadow Lake and Emily Lake passes Rosalie Lake and Gladys Lake in the heart of the Shadow Creek lake chain.

segments.trailName: John Muir Trail → Shadow Creek Trail → River Trail
segments.note: Backtrack the JMT past Shadow Lake and descend Shadow Creek to the River Trail junction — possible camp at Olaine Lake.

segments.trailName: River Trail → Pacific Crest Trail → Spooky Meadow Trail → Clark Lakes Trail
segments.note: North along the River Trail past Badger Lakes, then a quick Pacific Crest Trail link to Spooky Meadow. Finish through the Clark Lakes basin — Gem Lake, Clark Lakes, and Billy Lake along Rush Creek — back to the start.

pros: Only loop in the dataset that crosses both Donohue Pass and Island Pass — real above-treeline high-country experience
Donohue Peak visible from the pass approach, and Thousand Island Lake is one of the Sierra's most iconic backcountry destinations
Connects two distinct lake systems: the Rush Creek/Davis Lake chain in the north and the Shadow Lake/Rosalie chain in the south
48% JMT/PCT miles means well-maintained tread with reliable trail markers on the high passes
cons: At 43.7 miles, this is the longest of the three routes — day 1 (11.3 mi) and day 3 (14 mi) are demanding
Donohue Pass snow lingers into mid-July most years — ice axe and microspikes may be needed for the north-facing approach
The JMT corridor between Shadow Lake and Thousand Island Lake carries heavy thru-hiker traffic in July
Rush Creek trailhead requires driving to Silver Lake or June Lake from Bishop — about 1.5 hours with no direct Highway 395 access
gearTips: Microspikes and trekking poles for Donohue Pass snow — check recent trip reports before departing in early July
Bear canister required — camps at Thousand Island Lake and Shadow Lake attract bears
Sun protection critical above treeline on the Island Pass and Donohue Pass approaches — minimal shade for 5+ miles
Extra water capacity for the exposed Donohue Pass climb — no reliable water for 3 miles on the north side

name: Iron Creek & North Fork Wilderness Loop
summary: A 44.3-mile loop through the least-traveled southwest corner of the Ansel Adams Wilderness, following Iron Creek and the North Fork San Joaquin River drainage before swinging east through the Holcomb Lake and Ashley Lake basins. With only 5% of miles on the JMT/PCT, this route passes through terrain most Sierra backpackers never see — Granite Stairway, Iron Mountain, and a web of streams including Cargyle Creek, Dike Creek, and Slide Creek.
bestFor: Backpackers seeking solitude who want to explore the roadless interior beyond the JMT corridor, and don't mind longer days on less-maintained trail.

segments.trailName: Mammoth Trail → Snake Meadow Trail → Iron Creek Trail
segments.note: Start on the Mammoth Trail and drop into Snake Meadow, then descend the Iron Creek Trail along Dike Creek and Iron Creek to the North Fork San Joaquin River. This 5-mile stretch through the Iron Creek drainage is some of the most remote trail in the wilderness — expect downed trees and faint tread.

segments.trailName: Stevenson Trail → Iron Creek Trail
segments.note: Brief connector at the Stevenson Trail junction along Slide Creek.

segments.trailName: Iron Creek Trail → Mammoth Trail
segments.note: Climb back up Iron Creek past the Cargyle Creek confluence, then connect to the Mammoth Trail and ascend to Granite Stairway — a distinctive granite formation visible for miles. East Fork Cargyle Creek parallels the trail.

segments.trailName: Summit Meadow Trail → Summit Meadow - Holcomb Cutoff → Anona Lake Trail
segments.note: Cross Summit Meadow past Granite Stairway and Fern Lake, then take the out-and-back spur to Anona Lake — a quiet alpine tarn rarely visited. The Summit Meadow - Holcomb Cutoff descends along King Creek to the Holcomb Lake basin.

segments.trailName: Ashley Lake Trail → Holcomb Lake Trail
segments.note: Out-and-back to Ashley Lake, Holcomb Lake, and Noname Lake beneath Iron Mountain — three lakes in a granite pocket with no crowds. Red Top Mountain comes into view on the descent.

segments.trailName: Superior Lake Trail → John Muir Trail
segments.note: Follow the Superior Lake Trail — a long, gradual descent with Red Top Mountain behind you — to the brief John Muir Trail section near Johnston Lake and Minaret Creek.

segments.trailName: John Muir Trail → King Creek Trail → Summit Meadow Trail → Mammoth Trail
segments.note: Cross the Middle Fork San Joaquin River, then take King Creek Trail to Summit Meadow. The final stretch on the Mammoth Trail passes Granite Stairway and follows East Fork Cargyle Creek back to the start.

pros: Only 5% JMT/PCT miles — this is the most remote loop in the dataset with near-total solitude
12 distinct trail names — the highest trail variety of any route, covering terrain from deep forest to open granite
Three named peaks (Granite Stairway, Iron Mountain, Red Top Mountain) plus 6 named lakes — substantial features despite the remote location
9 named streams including the North Fork San Joaquin River, Iron Creek, and Cargyle Creek — reliable water sources throughout
cons: At 44.3 miles with day 1 and day 4 both over 10 miles, this is a strenuous route — not for those wanting a relaxed pace
Iron Creek Trail and Snake Meadow Trail see minimal maintenance — expect deadfall, overgrown sections, and route-finding challenges
No mountain pass crossings — the route stays below treeline, so above-treeline scenery is limited to the Granite Stairway and Iron Mountain viewpoints
The lakes (Anona, Ashley, Holcomb, Noname) are smaller and less dramatic than the Minarets lakes — this is a solitude route, not a postcard route
gearTips: GPS device or detailed topo map essential — Iron Creek Trail and Snake Meadow Trail have sparse signage and faint tread in places
Gaiters recommended for the overgrown sections along Iron Creek and Dike Creek in July
Bear canister required even in the remote sections — this area has active bears with fewer food-storage installations
Water filter needed despite abundant streams — cattle grazing occurs in the Iron Creek drainage
